g.data-narration: Welcome to Distributed Intrusion Detection in Cloud Computing. This topic explains how security evolves from traditional centralized approaches to distributed cloud-native solutions.
g.text: Distributed Intrusion Detection in Cloud Computing

g.data-narration: Traditional IDS monitors a central network point. But cloud environments are distributed with no single choke point, making centralized monitoring ineffective.
g.text: The shift from centralized to distributed security

g.data-narration: Centralized IDS fails in clouds due to five key reasons: no central choke point, dynamic VM migration, invisible east-west traffic, performance bottlenecks, and insider attacks.
g.text: Why Centralized IDS Fails in Cloud

g.data-narration: A Distributed IDS uses multiple agents: Host-based IDS inside VMs, Hypervisor IDS at the virtualization layer, Network IDS at switches, all reporting to a Global Manager.
Host-based IDS (HIDS) runs inside guest VMs, monitoring OS-level events like system calls, file integrity, and log files to detect local threats.
g.text: Detects local threats inside the VM

g.data-narration: Hypervisor-based IDS runs at the virtualization layer, monitoring VM behavior, network traffic between VMs on the same host, and resource usage patterns.
Network-based IDS (NIDS) monitors traffic at virtual switches and physical network perimeter, catching north-south traffic entering or leaving the data center.
g.text: Monitors North-South traffic (in out of data center)

g.data-narration: The Global IDS Manager correlates data from all agents, performing global correlation to identify coordinated attacks spanning multiple VMs that would be invisible to individual agents.
Signature-based detection uses known attack patterns from a database. It is effective against known threats with low false positives but cannot detect zero-day attacks.
Anomaly-based detection establishes normal behavior baselines and flags deviations. It can detect novel attacks but may generate false positives from legitimate behavior changes.
Key challenges include data collection overhead (network bandwidth), scalability (handling dynamic VM count), and correlation complexity across distributed sources.

g.text: High network usage - use filtering and aggregation

g.data-narration: Additional challenges: agent security (compromised agents can hide attacks), VM mobility (security context must follow migrating VMs), and false positives negatives.
Two deployment models exist: Cooperative (tenant-managed) offers control but lacks cross-tenant visibility, while Provider-centric offers broader visibility but requires trust.
g.text: Tenant deploys and manages own DIDS

g.data-narration: AWS GuardDuty is a real-world managed DIDS using VPC Flow Logs, CloudTrail, and DNS logs with machine learning to detect threats like crypto mining and data exfiltration.
Key takeaways: DIDS addresses cloud challenges with multi-layer agents (HIDS, Hypervisor, NIDS) reporting to a GIDM, using signature and anomaly detection while managing overhead and correlation.

g.text: DIDS addresses VM mobility and east-west traffic

g.data-narration: This concludes our exploration of Distributed Intrusion Detection in Cloud Computing. Remember to review the challenges and real-world applications for exam success.
